name: Longship
description: A long, narrow warship built for speed under both oar and sail. The shallow draft let viking crews land on beaches and travel up rivers other vessels couldn't reach.

name: Carved Dragon-Head Prow
description: An ornately carved dragon or serpent head mounted on a longship's prow. Meant to frighten enemies and ward off hostile spirits, it was often removed when approaching friendly shores.

name: Wooden Oar
description: A long-bladed pine or spruce oar pulled through ports along a longship's hull. When the wind failed, oar power was what made viking raids possible at all.

name: Sun Compass
description: A wooden disc with a central gnomon used to find true north by the shadow it cast. Combined with sunstones and dead reckoning, it helped viking navigators cross open ocean to Iceland and beyond.

name: Anchor Stone
description: A heavy holed stone lashed to a rope and dropped overboard to hold a ship in place. Cheap, replaceable, and effective in the shallow harbours where vikings beached and traded.

name: Ulfberht Sword
description: A high-carbon crucible-steel sword forged in the Rhineland, prized far above local ironwork. The +VLFBERHT+ inlay marked an early luxury brand.

name: Dane Axe
description: A two-handed long-hafted axe with a broad, thin blade. Devastating against shield walls and the signature weapon of viking huscarls.

name: Spear
description: The most common viking weapon — cheap, versatile, and effective. Spears were thrown, thrust in formation, or used in single combat with a shield.

name: Seax Knife
description: A single-edged utility knife worn by nearly every viking, free or thrall. Used for eating, working leather, and as a sidearm when nothing better was at hand.

name: Round Shield
description: A round plank shield with an iron boss covering the central handgrip. Light enough to swing offensively, it was the workhorse of the viking shield wall.

name: Spangenhelm
description: A conical iron helmet built from riveted plates, often with a nasal guard. Despite popular myth, viking helmets had no horns.

name: Chainmail Hauberk
description: A shirt of interlocking iron rings worn over padding. Expensive and labor-intensive, reserved for wealthy warriors and chieftains.

name: Drinking Horn
description: A polished cattle or aurochs horn used for ale, mead, and ceremonial toasts. Often rimmed with silver or bronze on the cups of wealthy hosts.

name: Hnefatafl Board
description: A strategy board game of unequal sides: a king and his defenders trying to escape a larger surrounding force. Played across the viking world long before chess arrived.

name: Antler Dice
description: Small cubes carved from antler or bone with drilled pip dots. Used for gambling games that the sagas warn could end in serious blood feuds.

name: Spindle Whorl
description: A weighted stone or clay disc that kept a hand spindle turning while spinning wool into yarn. The endless work of viking women, carried on at every spare moment.

name: Soapstone Bowl
description: A bowl carved from soft Norwegian soapstone, prized for resisting heat and cracking. A major Norwegian export traded as far as Britain and Ireland.

name: Quern Stone
description: A pair of round stones turned by hand to grind barley and rye into flour. Daily grinding produced the bread that fed every viking household.

name: Iron Firesteel and Flint
description: A curved iron striker paired with a flint nodule to throw sparks into tinder. Carried in a pouch by anyone who expected to spend a night away from a hearth.

name: Bone Flute
description: A simple flute cut from a hollow long-bone, usually sheep or goose. Common across viking-age settlements as everyday music for feasts and quiet evenings.

name: Fur-Lined Coat
description: A heavy woollen overcoat lined with fox, bear, or sheepskin. Essential against North Atlantic winters and a visible sign of wealth and trade reach.

name: Leather Turn-Shoe
description: A soft leather shoe stitched inside-out and then turned right-side-in to hide the seams. The standard footwear of farmer, trader, and raider alike.

name: Thor's Hammer Pendant
description: A small silver or iron pendant shaped like the hammer of Thor. Worn for protection, and possibly as a pagan answer to the cross as Christianity spread north.

name: Glass Trading Beads
description: Colourful glass beads strung between the brooches of a woman's apron-dress. Many came from as far as the Middle East via the Volga trade routes.

name: Cloak Brooch
description: A penannular or ring brooch used to pin a heavy wool cloak at the shoulder. Both practical fastener and showpiece, often elaborately decorated.

name: Silver Neck-Ring (Torc)
description: A twisted silver torc worn around the neck. Both jewellery and portable wealth — pieces could be cut off as hack-silver to pay for goods.

name: Bone Comb
description: A double-sided comb made from riveted antler plates. Combs were a near-universal personal item — the vikings were famously fastidious about their hair and beards.

name: Bone Needle Case
description: A small hollow bone tube used to store iron sewing needles. Often worn on a cord at the belt so a household's most easily lost tools were always to hand.
